questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the recommended frequency for replacing a pair of running shoes used by a regular distance runner for injury prevention?

options.text: Only when the shoes physically break apart
Every 500–800 km of running approximately
Once every five years without mileage checks
Never — running shoes last indefinitely

questions.explanation: Midsole cushioning in running shoes breaks down between about 500 and 800 kilometres; timely replacement maintains shock absorption and reduces overuse injuries such as stress fractures, plantar fasciitis and shin splints in distance runners.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Waiting for visible break-up invites overuse injuries.
Five-year intervals ignore cumulative mileage wear.
No sports shoe lasts indefinitely under regular use.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate clothing-related consideration for a footballer training outdoors during hot humid afternoons in summer?

options.text: Heavy dark-coloured sweat suit
Light, loose, moisture-wicking clothing with cap
Thick wool jersey and long trousers
Tight rubberised sauna suit

questions.explanation: Light-coloured loose clothing of moisture-wicking fabric allows convective heat loss and evaporation of sweat, while a cap shields the head from solar radiation; this combination reduces risk of heat cramps, exhaustion and heatstroke.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Dark heavy sweat suits trap heat dangerously.
Wool and long trousers impede heat dissipation.
Rubberised sauna suits cause severe dehydration risk.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is considered the most effective primary strategy to prevent heat-related illness in athletes during prolonged outdoor competition in hot climates?

options.text: Complete fasting throughout the competition day
Proper hydration, acclimatisation and heat-index scheduling
Wearing multiple layers of thick clothing throughout
Avoiding any shade or rest breaks during play

questions.explanation: Heat-illness prevention rests on pre-event hydration, gradual heat acclimatisation over 10–14 days, scheduling matches during cooler parts of the day, frequent cooling breaks, and monitoring the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature index for safe play conditions.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Fasting during heat exposure worsens dehydration risk.
Thick layers block evaporative heat loss.
Avoiding rest breaks accelerates heat-illness onset.

questions.question_text: A coach who carefully follows specific instructions and game rules for young athletes primarily contributes to injury prevention through which of the following channels?

options.text: Creating chaos that increases adrenaline release
Reducing unsafe actions through structure, technique and discipline
Ignoring any foul play to keep the game flowing
Encouraging high-risk plays to boost spectator interest

questions.explanation: Clear instructions on safe technique, strict adherence to rules and disciplined conduct reduce reckless actions, teach controlled skill execution, and create an environment where athletes use protective techniques — all lowering the incidence of preventable injuries.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Creating chaos increases rather than decreases injury.
Ignoring foul play directly raises injury rates.
Encouraging high-risk plays endangers athletes.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most important role of a well-balanced training programme that systematically alternates hard and easy sessions across the week?

options.text: It prevents any physical fitness gains from occurring
It allows tissue recovery and reduces overtraining risk
It guarantees a lifetime injury-free career
It makes athletes weaker than untrained individuals

questions.explanation: Alternating hard and easy sessions — the 'hard–easy principle' — gives muscles, tendons and bones time to repair micro-damage and adapt; this balanced approach reduces overtraining, cumulative overuse injury and burnout while improving performance.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Balanced programmes enhance, not prevent, adaptations.
No programme can guarantee lifetime injury freedom.
Training builds rather than reduces athletic capacity.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate action for a physical education teacher who notices a chipped wooden cricket stump before a school practice session begins?

options.text: Continue using it to save time and money
Remove it from use and replace with an undamaged stump
Cover damage with tape and continue using it
Ask the youngest student to use it first

questions.explanation: Damaged equipment creates sharp edges and unpredictable rebound behaviour that can cause lacerations, splinter injuries and impact trauma; safe practice demands immediate removal and replacement rather than makeshift repairs with tape.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Using damaged equipment transfers risk to students.
Tape cannot restore structural integrity safely.
Assigning risk to younger students is unethical.

questions.question_text: In the Van Mechelen four-step sequence of injury prevention research, what logically follows after establishing the extent and nature of the sports injury problem?

options.text: Implementing preventive measures without any evaluation
Identifying risk factors and injury mechanisms in detail
Concluding the research process permanently
Marketing the sport to a wider audience

questions.explanation: Van Mechelen's four-step model moves from establishing the injury problem, to identifying aetiology and mechanism, to introducing preventive measures, and finally to re-assessing injury rates in a repeat study cycle for continuous improvement.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Implementing measures without evaluation bypasses evidence.
Concluding prematurely defeats preventive research.
Marketing is unrelated to the research sequence.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the best rationale for including specific proprioceptive and balance training in an injury prevention programme for basketball players?

options.text: It distracts players from thinking about opponents
It improves joint position awareness and reactive neuromuscular control
It is primarily meant to entertain the coaching staff
It has no evidence base in sports medicine literature

questions.explanation: Proprioceptive and balance drills such as single-leg standing, wobble-board work and hop-stop tasks sharpen joint position sense and reactive muscle activation around the ankle and knee, reducing the incidence of ankle sprains and ACL injuries.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Distraction from opponents is not a training rationale.
Entertainment of staff is clearly not the purpose.
Proprioceptive training has strong evidence support.

questions.question_text: Which of the following measures most effectively reduces the risk of concussion in a rugby player during tackles, according to current sports-medicine evidence on head injury?

options.text: Completely banning any head contact and enforcing tackle-height rules
Forbidding the use of any mouth guard or head gear
Encouraging head-first charging technique for impact
Removing all medical supervision during matches

questions.explanation: Enforcing low tackle-height rules, red-carding deliberate head contact, graduated return-to-play after concussion, and education on safe tackling technique are the pillars of concussion prevention; helmets and mouth guards help but do not replace rule enforcement.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Banning protective gear worsens head injury risk.
Head-first technique is a major cause of concussion.
Removing medical supervision contradicts all safety guidelines.

questions.question_text: Which of the following nutritional practices most directly contributes to the long-term prevention of stress fractures in female distance runners with low menstrual regularity?

options.text: Adequate energy availability, calcium and vitamin D intake
Severe caloric restriction alongside heavy weekly mileage
Zero dairy and zero plant-calcium sources
Complete avoidance of sunlight exposure year-round

questions.explanation: Low energy availability, disordered eating and menstrual dysfunction together suppress bone mineral density (RED-S / Female Athlete Triad); adequate calories, calcium and vitamin D sustain bone remodelling, directly reducing stress-fracture incidence in female runners.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Caloric restriction plus heavy mileage weakens bone.
Eliminating all calcium sources accelerates bone loss.
Avoiding sunlight depletes vitamin D synthesis.

questions.question_text: Match each injury prevention measure in Column A with the most appropriate category it belongs to in Column B and choose the correct mapping option.
questions.explanation: Warm-ups fall under athlete preparation; playing-surface care belongs to environment and equipment; tackle-height rules sit under rules and officiating; pre-participation examination is part of medical supervision — a clean four-pillar match.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Warm-ups are athlete-preparation interventions.
Playing surface is always an environmental factor.
Rule enforcement is officiating-related.

questions.question_text: Match each recovery or regeneration strategy in Column A with its primary physiological benefit listed in Column B and choose the correct combination below.
questions.explanation: Deep sleep peaks growth-hormone release and tissue repair; balanced nutrition restocks glycogen, fluids and electrolytes; cool-down jogs gradually clear metabolic waste; weekly rest days allow cumulative remodelling and overall adaptation.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Sleep drives nightly hormonal tissue repair cycles.
Nutrition replaces the substrates used during training.
Rest days accumulate long-term adaptation.

questions.question_text: Match each common unsafe practice in Column A with the specific injury it most often leads to from Column B and select the correct combination from the options.

column_a.text: Sprinting without warm-up in cold weather

questions.explanation: Flooded fields cause slip-related sprains and falls; undersized helmets poorly absorb impact, raising concussion risk; rounded-spine lifting stresses lumbar discs; cold-start sprints tear tight hamstrings — each unsafe practice maps to a typical injury.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Flooded surfaces mainly cause slip and ankle injuries.
Rounded-spine lifting overloads lumbar intervertebral discs.
Cold unwarmed hamstrings are classic sprint-strain victims.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason regarding hydration in injury prevention and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in sport.
questions.assertion: Adequate hydration before and during long outdoor training sessions reduces the risk of muscle cramps and heat illness.
questions.reason: Fluid and electrolyte maintenance supports sweat-based thermoregulation and neuromuscular function during exercise.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Fluid loss through sweat reduces plasma volume and raises core temperature; electrolyte loss disturbs neuromuscular excitability causing cramps; so maintaining both supports thermoregulation and muscle function, directly preventing cramps and heat illness.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: The reason correctly explains the mechanism of the assertion.
Both statements are well-supported by exercise physiology.
Hydration's protective role against cramps is well-established.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason about neuromuscular training programmes in young female athletes and choose the option showing their correct logical connection.
questions.assertion: Regular neuromuscular training programmes significantly reduce non-contact ACL injury rates in adolescent female football and basketball players.
questions.reason: Such programmes guarantee complete lifetime immunity against all forms of knee injury in every sport setting.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: The assertion is supported by strong evidence showing 30–60% reductions in non-contact ACL injuries with programmes like FIFA 11+; however the reason is false — such programmes reduce but do not eliminate injury risk, and no programme guarantees lifetime immunity.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Evidence for ACL risk reduction is strong for these programmes.
No programme promises total lifetime immunity.
The reason overstates the protective effect.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason about cross-training in injury prevention and select the option that describes their logical relationship most accurately.
questions.assertion: Cross-training with low-impact activities like swimming and cycling reduces the risk of overuse injuries in runners.
questions.reason: Cross-training preserves cardiovascular fitness while varying mechanical load on muscles, joints and bones.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Cross-training maintains aerobic conditioning while shifting load patterns to different tissues, reducing cumulative mechanical stress from repetitive running; this redistribution directly explains why cross-training lowers overuse injury incidence in runners.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Both statements reflect established sports science.
The reason correctly explains the mechanism.
Load redistribution directly lowers overuse risk.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following stages of a graduated return-to-play protocol for an athlete recovering from a Grade I hamstring strain into the correct logical order used in sports medicine.

items.text: Rest, ice and initial RICE management

options.text: S → Q → P → R
Q → P → R → S
R → Q → S → P
P → S → R → Q

questions.explanation: Return-to-play progression for a Grade I hamstring strain starts with RICE, advances to pain-free active range of motion, then light jogging and functional work, and finally sport-specific drills at full intensity before match exposure.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: RICE is always the initial post-injury step.
Active movement must precede jogging and drills.
Sport-specific drills are the final stage before play.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following steps taken to prepare a school playground safely before a sports event into the correct logical pre-event sequence used by organisers.

items.text: Mark boundaries, lanes and safety zones clearly
Inspect the ground for holes, debris and hazards
Repair or remove any identified hazard
Brief staff, volunteers and first-aid team

options.text: Q → R → P → S
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

questions.explanation: Organisers first inspect the ground for hazards, then repair or remove identified risks, proceed to mark boundaries and safety zones, and finally brief staff, volunteers and first-aid teams on their roles and emergency procedures.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Inspection must precede marking any boundaries.
Hazard repair logically follows its identification.
Briefing staff is the final readiness step.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: An athlete playing through sharp severe pain always strengthens the body and never results in worsening of the existing injury.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Sharp severe pain is a warning signal of tissue damage; continuing to play through it typically worsens the injury, converting minor strains into severe tears or incomplete fractures into displaced fractures — never a safe approach.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Severe pain is a protective signal, not a strengthening cue.
Continuing play often converts minor to severe injuries.
Sports medicine consistently warns against playing through pain.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram showing the Van Mechelen four-step sequence of prevention of sports injury research; identify the step that immediately follows implementation of preventive measures in the cycle.

options.text: Establishing the extent of the injury problem
Identifying risk factors and injury mechanisms
Re-assessing injury incidence by repeating the first step
Stopping all further research activity permanently

questions.explanation: After introducing preventive measures, the Van Mechelen model re-assesses injury incidence by repeating step one; comparing before-and-after rates evaluates whether the intervention worked, closing the loop for evidence-based prevention.
questions.subject: Physical Education
elimination_hints: Extent establishment is the first, not follow-up step.
Risk factor identification precedes implementation.
Stopping research defeats the purpose of the cycle.
